coredata.dc:title: Boosting the kinetics with graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-decorated NiCo2O4 nanosheets towards high-performance Li-O2 batteries
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yu
Zhu, Xingbao
Wu, Yuanguo
Man, Zining
Wen, Xiangyu
Lü, Zhe
coredata.dc:description: The performance of energy-dense lithium-oxygen batteries depend primarily on the electrocatalytic activity and architecture of the oxygen electrodes. However, highly catalytically active cathode materials such as metal oxides suffer from insufficient electronic conductivity, resulting in restricted kinetics and O2/e−/Li+ three-phase reaction regions. In this study, we design and fabricate a free-standing and lightweight cathode with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) modified NiCo2O4 (NCO) nanosheets array anchored on 3D graphene foam (GF). The potential electrocatalytic activity of metal oxide materials can be fully excited by electronic conductivity compensation, and the triple-phase reaction regions are expanded from the NCO/GF interface to the entire surface of NCO. As a result, the cathode can deliver a discharge capacity as high as 7672 mAh g−1, cacaulated based on the whole mass of the entire cathode, and can sustain more than 500 cycles with a high energy efficiency of over 78.5%. These findings demonstrate that the GQDs@NCO@GF cathode has remarkable potential for applications in lithium-oxygen batteries, and the cathode structure design scheme and GQDs modification strategy can be further applied to other high-efficiency catalysts.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-oxygen batteries
Graphene quantum dots
NiCo2O4 nanosheets
3D graphene foam
Conductivity compensation

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22179030 ) and 21C Innovation Laboratory, Contemporary Amperex Technology Ltd (No. 21C-OP-202218).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images for (a) Ni foam and (b,c) GF, (d) Raman spectra of the GF.
Schematic diagram of the NCO@GF and GQDs@NCO@GF.
SEM images of (a,b) the NCO@GF and (c,d) GQDs@NCO@GF cathodes. (e) Schematic diagram for the effect of GQDs on morphology construction of NCO.

(a) TEM images of the GQDs; The cale bar in the inset represents 1 nm. (b) The PL excitation spectra and emission spectra of GQDs in aqueous solution; The PL excitation spectra is obtained by detecting fluorescence at 452 nm and the emission curve is obtained under the best excitation light of 363 nm. (c) XPS wide spectrum of GQDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of GQDs: (d) C1s, (e) N1s and (f) O1s. (g) XRD patterns of the GQDs@NCO@GF cathode. High-resolution XPS spectra of the GQDs@NCO@GF cathode: (h) Ni 2p, (i) Co 2p.

LSV curves measured in ORR region of the (a) GF, (b) NCO@GF, and (c) GQDs@NCO@GF at different rotating ranging speeds from 225 to 2025 rpm. (d) LSV curves of the OER process for the GF, NCO@GF, and GQDs@NCO@GF; (e) the corresponding Tafel plots. (f) Linear fitting of the corresponding current densities measured by CV at various scan rates to estimate the C dl from the slope.

(a) Nyquist impedance plots of Li-O2 batteries with GF, NCO@GF and GQDs@NCO@GF cathodes at the open-circuit potential, inset: equivalent circuit. (b) Discharge–charge curves of the GF, NCO@GF, GQDs@NCO@GF cathodes at a constant current density of 0.1 mA cm−2. Cycle performance of the GQDs@NCO@GF cathode at 0.1 mA cm−2 with a limited specific capacity of 500 mAh g−1: (c) discharge–charge curves of the selected cycles; (d) corresponding terminal voltages and energy efficiencies.
SEM images of (a) the fully discharged, and (b) fully charged GQDs@NCO@GF cathode. (c) High-resolution Li 1 s spectra of the GQDs@NCO@GF cathode after full discharge.
Comparison for specific capacities of recently reported cathodes for Li-O2 batteries.

head.ce:title: Boosting the kinetics with graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-decorated NiCo2O4 nanosheets towards high-performance Li-O2 batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin, Heilongjiang 150080, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aSchool of Physics, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin, Heilongjiang 150080, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics, Beijing Institute of Technology, Beijing 100081, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bSchool of Physics, Beijing Institute of Technology, Beijing 100081, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Physics, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin, Heilongjiang 150080, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The performance of energy-dense lithium-oxygen batteries depend primarily on the electrocatalytic activity and architecture of the oxygen electrodes. However, highly catalytically active cathode materials such as metal oxides suffer from insufficient electronic conductivity, resulting in restricted kinetics and O2/e−/Li+ three-phase reaction regions. In this study, we design and fabricate a free-standing and lightweight cathode with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) modified NiCo2O4 (NCO) nanosheets array anchored on 3D graphene foam (GF). The potential electrocatalytic activity of metal oxide materials can be fully excited by electronic conductivity compensation, and the triple-phase reaction regions are expanded from the NCO/GF interface to the entire surface of NCO. As a result, the cathode can deliver a discharge capacity as high as 7672 mAh g−1, cacaulated based on the whole mass of the entire cathode, and can sustain more than 500 cycles with a high energy efficiency of over 78.5%. These findings demonstrate that the GQDs@NCO@GF cathode has remarkable potential for applications in lithium-oxygen batteries, and the cathode structure design scheme and GQDs modification strategy can be further applied to other high-efficiency catalysts.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Rechargeable lithium-oxygen (Li-O2) batteries possess an ultra-high theoretical energy density of 11,140 Wh kg−1 (excluding oxygen mass) and hold promise as a candidate for next-generation green energy storage systems [1–3]. Despite many breakthroughs over the last few decades, Li-O2 batteries are still a long way from being practical. The slow kinetics of oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) and oxygen evolution reaction (OER) lead to many critical issues including high discharge–charge overpotential, poor rate performance, short cycle life, and low energy efficiency [4–6]. It has been demonstrated that the material selection and structural design of the oxygen electrode are two key factors that determine the electrochemical performance of Li-O2 batteries [7,8].

Carbon or carbon-based materials have been the primary choice of cathode materials for Li-O2 batteries owing to their high electronic conductivity, abundant catalytic active sites, especially light weight, which corresponds to high specific capacity [9–11]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are the latest member of the nanocarbon materials system, exhibiting unique properties such as bandgap expansion owing to quantum confinement, more plentiful active sites including edges, functional groups, dopants, etc., efficient electron transfer, and high electrocatalytic properties [12–14]. In the past few years, GQDs have gained great interest for new application opportunities in many fields, including bio-imaging, photodetectors, photocatalysis, and electrocatalysis [15,16]. Our previous work first reported the application of GQDs as a cathode catalyst for Li-O2 batteries [17], which displayed remarkable catalytic activity for the ORR and yielded an ultrahigh capacity of 68,900 mA h g−1 calculated based on the weight of GQDs. The capacity increased by GQDs load is limited, however, because individual isolated GQDs deposited on conducting surfaces tend to restack, diminishing the advantage of large edges and decreasing the availability of active sites. Once the weight of the cathode skeleton is taken into account, the battery's capacity is no longer advantageous. In addition, the OER catalytic activity for carbon materials towards Li2O2 decomposition is relatively low [18,19]. Various catalysts for Li-O2 batteries have been investigated in order to improve catalytic activity and reduce overpotential. Among them, NiCo2O4 (NCO) with ternary spinel structure has attracted widespread attention as a catalyst for Li-O2 batteries due to excellent bifunctional catalytic activity toward the ORR and OER, low cost and higher electronic conductivity than Co3O4 and NiO [20]. Although enhanced electrocatalytical performances of NCO have been reported, the capacity and charge-discharge overpotential are far from satisfactory, which may be largely due to the limited kinetics caused by low electronic conductivity and suboptimal structural design [21–23].

The structure of cathode is crucial to the electrochemical behavior of Li-O2 batteries as the main place where O2, electrons, and Li+ ions react to produce Li2O2 [24]. The solid-state product Li2O2 tends to accumulate in the pores on the air side of the cathode, which gradually blocks the oxygen pathway and terminates the discharge process prior to the pore volume and surface area of the cathode being completely consumed, limiting the actual discharge capacity [25,26]. In order to overcome this challenge, the design of the cathode structure requires the construction of criss-crossing holes with diameters significantly larger than the size of the solid-sate products. According to previous reports, the research of cathode design mainly focuses on the porous 3D skeletons and high surface area [27–30], where active materials are mixed with binders and coated onto 3D skeletons. These 3D skeletons are usually thick and porous materials with high electronic conductivity, such as carbon paper, carbon cloth and nickel foam. Generally, the mass of the cathode skeleton is not taken into account when calculating the specific capacity of the battery, even though it accounts for 90% of the total weight of the cathode, obviously, which is very unfavorable for commercial applications. Moreover, organic binder materials are usually unstable in the presence of reactive oxygen species, such as O2 ‒ [31,32].

In this work, we design and fabricate a binder-free and lightweight composite cathode in which NCO nanosheets array serves as a superior bifunctional catalyst, and free-standing graphene foam (GF) serves as 3D cathode skeleton. NCO nanosheets array are fabricated by a hydrothermal treatment, which not only enhances the ORR and OER catalytic activity, but also provides sufficient space for electrochemical reactions and products deposition. Although NCO nanosheets array has the ability to provide more active sites, the O2/e−/Li+ triple-phase reaction active regions are still confined to the contact interface of NCO nanosheets and graphene skeleton due to the low electronic conductivity of transition-metal oxides. Furthermore, we implement functional modifications with GQDs to construct electron transport pathways onto the surface of NCO nanosheets, which solves the key issue of NCO and is expected to fully excite its potential thermodynamic activity. Our results provide evidence of enhanced performance in the discharge–charge overpotential and specific capacity of Li-O2 batteries containing the GQDs@NCO@GF composite cathodes. The rational cathode structure and GQDs modification strategy are expected to find numerous promising applications for Li-O2 batteries.

3D GF was prepared by chemical vapor deposition (CVD). The commercial nickel foam substrates were punched into 15 mm diameter wafers, which were cleaned with acetone and alcohol in sequence. The nickel foam wafers were then transferred into a quartz tube furnace, where 100 sccm of Ar and 20 sccm of H2 were introduced and heated to 900 ℃, followed by 10 sccm of C2H2 for 1 min to deposit graphene. Free-standing 3D GF was finally obtained after etching away the nickel substrate with nitric acid.

The GQDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal method. 0.1 g of citric acid monohydrate, 300 µL of ethylenediamine, and 500 µL of deionized water were added to a Teflon-lined reaction kettle and heated at 110 °C for 4 h. After cooling to room temperature, 5 ml of deionized water was added to dissolve the product and form a clear solution. After adding a certain amount of methanol, precipitation of GQDs was observed. The GQDs were collected by centrifugation, then washed with methanol and dried. The prepared GQDs were readily redispersed into water.

1 mmol of Co(NO3)2·6H2O, 0.5 mmol of NiCl2·6H2O, 3 mmol of NH4F, 5 mmol of hexamethylenetetramine, and 7.5 mg GQDs were added in one portion to 15 mL of deionized water and stirred until a clear purple solution formed. The solution was then transferred to a Teflon-lined kettle with a piece of GF and heated at 120 °C for 6 h. After cooling to room temperature, the product was collected and annealed in air at 400° for 3 h. The mass loading of GQDs@NCO composites on GF was about 0.6 mg cm−2. As a comparison, we prepared NCO@GF composites without GQDs using the same method.

The scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi S-4700) and the transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100F) were used for morphology observation. The X-ray diffraction (XRD, PANalytical Aeris) was used to analyze the composition of the sample. The photoluminescence (PL) excitation spectra and emission spectra were measured on a spectrometer (Edinburgh FLS1000). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) characterization was performed by using a Thermo Scientific K-alpha multi-technique system. Raman spectra was recorded with a microscopic confocal Raman spectrometer (Nanobase XperRam Compact) with a 532 nm laser beam. The rotating disk electrode (RDE) analysis was performed on an electrochemical test system (ALS RRDE-3A) with a saturated calomel electrode (in saturated KCl solution) and a Pt wire used as reference and counter electrodes.

Li-O2 batteries composed of commercial lithium metal foil (Tansworld Enterprises Ltd., China), glass fiber separator (Whatman GF/C, America), as-prepared cathode and liquid electrolyte (1 M LiTFSI in TEGDME, Guangdong Canrd New Energy Technology Co., Ltd.) were assembled in an Ar-filled glove box (Braun, Germany) with water and oxygen content below 0.5 ppm. Li-O2 batteries were tested in pure oxygen using a Neware CT-3008 W battery test system.

The free-standing 3D GF was prepared by chemical vapor deposition (CVD) using commercial nickel foam as a rigid template, followed by etching of the nickel template with nitric acid. Compared with the structures constructed by stacking graphene nanosheets, the as-prepared GF is grown into a monolithic 3D network, which facilitates faster charge carrier transport. The morphology and micro-structure of the as-prepared GF are investigated by a scanning electron microscopy (SEM), as shown in Fig. 1 . The low magnification insets display the macroscopic 3D skeleton of the Ni foam (Fig. 1a) and GF (Fig. 1b), with pore diameters ranging from 50 to 200 μm. The GF completely retains the interlinked porous structure and 3D skeleton of the Ni foam to construct a hollow free-standing 3D GF, which is just around 1/400 the weight of the nickel foam. As shown in Fig. 1c, the GF has a uniform thickness with wrinkles and ripples on its surface. These special structures are caused by the different thermal expansion properties of graphene and nickel foam during CVD process. These structural characteristics increase the roughness, curvature, and mechanical properties of the GF, which is conducive to the adsorption of O2. Fig. 1d exhibites the Raman spectra of the GF, in which two distinct peaks are observed at 1586 cm−1 (G band) and 2724 cm−1 (2D band). The faint defect-induced peak (D band) is a characteristic of high-quality few-layer graphene [33].

Fig. 2 shows the hierarchical structure and schematic diagram of the NCO@GF and GQDs@NCO@GF cathodes. The 3D macroporous structure with pore diameters ranging from 50 to 200 μm of the GF facilitates the rapid diffusion of oxygen into the cathode. The 3D skeleton of GF is wrapped by NCO nanosheets with a thickness of less than 10 nm, which will provide abundant active sites for the ORR and OER, and sufficient space for the deposition of products. However, the big issue of NCO's poor electrical conductivity remains unanswered, which severely limits the release of NCO electrochemical activity and significantly restrict the active sites to the NCO and GF boundaries. After decorated with GQDs, the triple-phase reaction regions extend from boundaries to the whole surface of NCO nanosheets, and the kinetic performance of the cathode should be improved significantly due to the compensated electronic conductivity from GQDs. Notably, the surface of NCO is designed to be partially covered by GQDs, in order to expose the active sites of NCO as much as possible. The corresponding SEM images are presented in Fig. 3 , and the results show that the GQDs modified NCO and pure NCO are generally vertical nanosheets with uniform thickness but have different surface structures. Without the assistance from GQDs, the formed nanosheets with smaller lateral dimensions (2.5–5 μm) show wavy and loose structures (Fig. 3a, b). While the GQDs modified NCO nanosheets with larger lateral dimensions (5–10 μm) display flatter and denser structures (Fig. 3c, d). The above results may be attributed to the strong coordination ability of oxygenated functional groups such as carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on GQDs, which can form stable complexes with metal cations, thereby activating the substrate to generate more nucleation sites [34,35]. According to previous reports, similar mechanisms, such as the use of F− and metal cations to form complexes, have been applied to modulate the morphology of transition-metal oxide catalysts [36,37]. As shown in the schematic diagram (Fig. 3e), the presence of GQDs may stimulate substrates to generate more active nucleation sites and promote a stronger interaction between NCO and the substrate. During the formation of the nanosheets, the bonded GQDs on its surface will produce new nucleation sites, causing the nanosheets to expand laterally and form a larger size. This binding mechanism of GQDs and NCO is beneficial to promote the uniform distribution and firm attachment of GQDs on the NCO nanosheets.

The particle size and crystal structure of the as-prepared GQDs were characterized by the transmission electron microscopy (TEM), as shown in Fig. 4 a. The GQDs are monodisperse and uniformly distributed, with a particle size distribution of 1–4 nm (Fig. S1). The high-resolution TEM image (inset) displays clear lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.21 nm, matching to the (100) in-plane lattice of graphene [38]. The high-dispersion properties of the GQDs can fully expose their highly active boundaries. At the same time, when the GQDs are modified on the surface of NCO, the active sites of NCO can be exposed as much as possible, which verifies the feasibility of our design of high conductivity and high activity catalyst. The photoluminescence (PL) excitation spectra of the GQDs (Fig. 4b) reveals two characteristic peaks at 259 and 363 nm, which correspond to the π-π* transitions of the CC bonds in the aromatic rings and the CO bonds in the functional groups, respectively [39,40]. The PL emission spectra of the GQDs display a characteristic peak centered at 452 nm under the optimal excitation light of 363 nm. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra are employed to further investigate the surface states of the GQDs. The wide spectra in Fig. 4c shows three characteristic peaks: C 1 s (285.1 eV), N 1 s (400.4 eV), and O 1 s (531.9 eV), with corresponding atomic contents of 65.76%, 9.42%, and 24.81%, respectively. These findings indicate that the GQDs are rich in oxygenated functional groups and doped with nitrogen atoms. The high-resolution spectra presented in Fig. 4d reveals that the C 1 s band contains three peaks at 284.6, 286.2, and 288.0 eV, representing CC, CO/CN, and CO, respectively [41]. The N 1 s band contains three peaks at 398.9, 400.3, and 401.3 eV, corresponding to pyridinic-N, pyrrolic-N, and graphitic-N, respectively [42]. The O 1 s band can be deconvoluted into two peaks at 531.6 and 532.5 eV, representing CO and CO, respectively (Fig. 4f) [43]. The oxygenated functional groups not only facilitate the nucleation and growth process of NCO nanosheets, but also endow GQDs with intrinsic catalytic properties as redox active sites. Doped nitrogen atoms can significantly modify the electronic structures of GQDs and influence their electron transfer characteristics [44]. For instance, N-doped GQDs display notably enhanced electrocatalytic activity for the ORR [45].

The compositions and structures of the obtained GQDs@NCO@GF cathode are characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD) and XPS. Fig. 4g reveals the XRD patterns for the obtained GQDs@NCO@GF cathode. All of the diffraction peaks at 18.9°, 31.1°, 36.7°, 38.4°, 44.6°, 55.4°, 59.1°, and 65.0° are identified to be the (111), (220), (311), (222), (400), (422), (511), and (440) crystal planes of NCO, respectively (JCPDS No. 20–0781). There are no distinct peaks that can be attributed to GQDs probably due to the relatively low crystallinity of GQDs. XPS was used to further investigate the surface structure of the GQDs@NCO@GF cathode. The full XPS spectra in Fig. S2a confirms the coexistence of Ni, Co, O, N, and C in the GQDs@NCO@GF cathode. The energy dispersive spectrum (EDS) results in Fig. S3 further confirm the homogeneous distribution of Ni, Co, O, N, and C elements. The high-resolution Ni 2p spectrum consists of two characteristic spin-orbit doublets ascribed to Ni2+ and Ni3+, and two shake-up satellites (Fig. 4h). Specifically, the fitted peaks at 854.6 and 872.0 eV correspond to Ni2+, and the peaks at 856.2 and 873.8 eV correspond to Ni3+, respectively. Similarly, the high-resolution Co 2p spectrum in Fig. 4i exhibits that the fitted peaks at 779.5 and 794.2 eV correspond to Co3+, and the fitted peaks at 781.7 and 795.7 eV correspond to Co2+. These findings suggest that the chemical composition of NCO nanosheets consists Ni2+, Ni3+, Co2+ and Co3+, which is in good agreement with the literature results, and may provide good electrocatalytic activity for the ORR and OER [46,47]. The high-resolution C 1 s spectrum of the GQDs@NCO@GF cathode in Fig. S2b can be deconvoluted into three different peaks at 284.7, 285.3, and 288.9 eV, representing CC, CO/CN, and CO, respectively. The high-resolution N 1 s spectrum in Fig. S2c contains three peaks at 398.2, 399.6, and 401.1 eV, corresponding to pyridinic-N, pyrrolic-N, and graphitic-N, respectively. The high-resolution O 1 s spectrum in Fig. S2d can be deconvoluted into four different peaks at 529.5, 531.3, 532.4, and 533.7 eV, corresponding to the typical metal-oxygen bonds, CO, CO, and defect sites lacking of oxygen coordination [48]. These results further confirm that the GQDs are distributed on the surface of the NCO nanosheets and have abundant organic functional groups. Such composite structure can facilitate charge transfer and significantly accelerate the kinetic process of the electrode reaction, which is required for low discharge-charge overpotentials.

Intrinsic catalytic activity of the GQDs@NCO@GF sample was evaluated on a rotating disk electrode by recording linear sweep voltammograms (LSV) in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH at various rotating speeds from 225 to 2025 rpm. The GF and NCO@GF samples are tested as control samples. Fig. 5 a–c demonstrates that the onset potential of the GF is 0.66 V (vs RHE) and the half-wave potential (E1/2) is 0.37 V, while the corresponding values for the NCO@GF are 0.69 and 0.55 V, respectively. Both of the potentials for the GQDs@NCO@GF are more positive, located at 0.71 and 0.58 V. Furthermore, the limiting current density of the ORR on the GQDs@NCO@GF is consistently higher than that of the NCO@GF and GF at each rotation rate. The improved onset potential, half-wave potential, and limiting current density indicate an enhanced catalytic ability for the ORR process [49]. Fig. 5d exhibits the OER performance of the cathodes at 1600 rpm. The GF sample displays poor OER performance with a limiting current density of 1.7 mA cm−2 and an onset potential of 1.84 V. While the limiting current density for the OER on the NCO@GF is much higher than that of the GF with a value of 10 mA cm−2 and an onset potential of 1.62 V. The GQDs@NCO@GF sample exhibited the highest limiting current density of 11 mA cm−2 with an improved onset potential of 1.61 V, indicating enhanced catalytic activity for the OER. In addition, the Tafel slope of the GQDs@NCO@GF sample is 82 mV/decede, which is smaller than that of the NCO@GF (90 mV/decade) and GF (181 mV/decade) (Fig. 5e), suggesting more rapid kinetics for the OER. The electrocatalytically active area (ECSA) in the GQDs@NCO@GF, NCO@GF, and GF, their ECSAs were measured via cyclic voltammetry (CV) test (Fig. S4a–c). The current response in potential range of 0.95 to 1.05 V at varied scan rates in the range of 20–120 mV s−1 corresponds to the double-layer capacitance (C dl), which is proportional to the ECSA [50]. Fig. 5f demonstrates that the C dl of the NCO@GF is 1.74 mF cm−2, about tenfold that of the GF. The C dl of the GQDs@NCO@GF is 2.26 mF cm−2, which is about 30% higher than that of the NCO@GF. The above results indicate that NCO exhibits high thermodynamic activity for both oxygen reduction and oxygen evolution reactions, and the GQDs modification can effectively improve the performance of the catalyst by accelerating the kinetic processes related to mass transfer.

As shown in Fig. 6 a, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) tests were utilized to further investigate the kinetics behavior of the GF, NCO@GF and GQDs@NCO@GF cathodes. The analytical equivalent circuit model is shown in the inset. R0 is related to the ohmic resistance, including contact resistance and intrinsic resistance of the electrolyte and electrodes, and its value corresponds to the high frequency intercept on the real axis. R1 and CPE1 are related to the charge transfer resistance at anode and cathode interfaces. The fitted value of the resistances for different cathodes are displayed in Table 1 . For the samples GF, NCO@GF and GQDs@NCO@GF, the fitted values of ohmic resistances (R0 ) are 3.34, 9.35 and 4.86 Ω, respectively, indicating that the NCO nanosheets coating layer brings in extra electronic resistance, and GQDs modification can effectively compensate the electronic conductance of the NCO nanosheets. The charge transfer resistances (R1 ) for NCO@GF and GQDs@NCO@GF are lower than that of GF, revealing that NCO nanosheets can extend the electrolyte-cathode interface. The lowest R1 for GQDs@NCO@GF indicates that GQDs make a positive effect on accelerating the kinetics at the interface between electrolyte and cathode.

The discharge-charge properties of the GF, NCO@GF and GQDs@NCO@GF cathodes at 0.1 mA cm−2 (≈150 mA g−1 based on the GQDs@NCO@GF cathode) are compared in Fig. 6b. For the GF cathode, a capacity of 717 mAh g−1 is delivered with a discharge overpotential of 0.44 V, while the NCO@GF cathode exhibites a significant increase of 471% to 4091 mAh g−1 with a lower discharge overpotential of 0.42 V. The capacity enhancement is attributed to the larger surface area provided by the NCO nanosheets for electrochemical reactions and product deposition. Notably, the charge overpotential of the NCO@GF cathode is dramatic reduced to 0.98 V, which is 0.63 V lower than that of the GF cathode, attributed to the enhanced the OER catalytic activity of NCO. Compared with the NCO@GF cathode, the GQDs@NCO@GF cathode shows a 88% further capacity enhancement reaching 7672 mAh g−1. The significant capacity increase is attributed to the expansion of triple-phase reaction active regions from the boundarys between the graphene and NCO nanosheets to the entire cathode with the help of the GQDs to promote charge transfer. It is worth emphasizing that the specific capacity achieved in this work is calculated according to the total mass of the cathode, whereas most current reports are based on the mass of the active materials without counting the substrate or even the binder. However, the substrate or current collector generally accounts for most of the weight of the cathode, so excluding the performance of the substrate is convenient for the comparative evaluation of active materials, but has little value for practical application. Table 2 displays comprehensive comparison about the reported 3D porous cathodes for Li-O2 batteries. Once the weight of substrates such as nickel foam, carbon paper and carbon cloth are taken into account, the specific capacity of most reported cathodes is significantly reduced to less than 1000 mAh g−1. In our work, GQDs modified NCO nanosheets are anchored to an ultra-light 3D graphene skeleton to form a free-standing structure that greatly reduces the overall weight of the cathode and achieves ultra-high specific capacity. In addition, the charge overpotential of the GQDs@NCO@GF cathode is further reduced to 0.93 V. The lower voltage polarization during charging is caused by the enhanced OER kinetics of the GQDs@NCO@GF cathode, which is beneficial to the increase of energy efficiency, the suppression of side reactions, and the extension for cycle life.

The cycling stability of the GQDs@NCO@GF cathode was investigated by operating a non-aqueous Li-O2 battery with a fixed capacity of 500 mA h g−1 and a constant current density of 0.1 mA cm−2. As shown in Fig. 6c, the Li-O2 battery can sustain stably more than 500 cycles with a steady terminal voltage. The initial discharge voltage plateau is about 2.61 V, and the charge voltage plateau is 3.84 V with a high energy efficiency of 80.1%. After 500 cycles, the terminal discharge voltage drops to 2.55 V and the charge voltage climbs to 4.04 V, and the energy efficiency can be maintained at 78.5% (Fig. 6d). The stable cycle performance of the GQDs@NCO@GF cathode demonstrates its promising application for Li-O2 batteries.

To further investigate the reversibility of Li2O2 formation and decomposition, the fully discharged and fully charged GQDs@NCO@GF cathodes were characterized by SEM, as shown in Fig. 7 . After full discharge (Fig. 7a), the surface of the NCO nanosheets was uniformly covered with a high-density distribution of solid product Li2O2, confirming that the GQDs extended the triple-phase reaction regions to the entire cathode. After full charge (Fig. 7b), no obvious Li2O2 nanoparticles were observed on the surface of the NCO nanosheets, indicating good reversibility of the electrode reaction. In addition, XPS characterization present in Fig. 7c was employed to establish further confirm the composition of the product. The high-resolution Li 1 s spectrum contains two peaks at 54.5 eV and 55.3 eV. The dominant peak at 54.5 eV is attributed to Li2O2, revealing Li2O2 is the primary discharge product. The second peak is attributed to lithium carbonate species, inferring that a certain amount of Li2O2 reacts with the electrolyte to evolve into Li2CO3 [63]. All these results demonstrate a favorable rechargeability of the GQDs@NCO@GF cathode.

In conclusion, we propose and fabricate a lightweight cathode for Li-O2 batteries based on GQDs modified NCO anchored on 3D GF scaffold. The GQDs modification strategy was demonstrated to an effective way that can both compensate the electronic conductivity of oxide catalysts and fully excite its potential electrocatalytic activity. Moreover, the highly active boundaries of GQDs were comprehensively exposed due to the large surface area of NCO (facilitating monolayer deposition) and the great dispersion properties of GQDs (avoiding aggregation). The analysis of the EIS indicates that both the ohmic resistance and charge transfer resistance of the GQDs@NCO@GF cathode are significantly reduced compared with the NCO@GF cathode. The LSV results show that the GQDs@NCO@GF cathode has superior onset potential, half-wave potential and limiting current for the ORR and OER processes, corresponding to enhanced catalytic activity. Furthermore, the O2/e−/Li+ triple-phase reaction regions are enlarged from the NCO/GF interface to the entire surface of NCO. Therefore, the GQDs@NCO@GF cathode delivers an outstanding discharge capacity of 7672 mAh g−1 (88% higher than that of the NCO@GF cathode and 970% higher than that of the GF cathode) at 0.1 mA cm−2 with low charge overpotential of 0.93 V (0.05 V lower than that of the NCO@GF cathode and 0.68 V lower than that of the GF cathode). Notably, the 3D GF as a flexible cathode framework significantly reduces the total mass of the cathode. Compared with traditional cathode framework materials such as carbon paper, carbon cloth, and nickel foam, the discharge capacity of the GQDs@NCO@GF cathode is increased by more than ten times calculated on the whole mass of the entire cathode. The proposed cathode can sustain more than 500 cycles with a high energy efficiency of over 78.5%. These findings demonstrate that the GQDs modification strategy can effectively solve the bottleneck problem of limited kinetic activity of metal oxide catalysts due to insufficient electronic conductivity, and provide a new idea for the efficient utilization of metal oxide catalysts in the field of electrochemistry. The proposed composite cathode would promote the potential application of high-energy-density Li-O2 batteries in the field of energy storage.

Yu Wang: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Xingbao Zhu: Validation, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Yuanguo Wu: Software, Writing – review & editing. Zining Man: Methodology, Investigation. Xiangyu Wen: Methodology, Investigation. Zhe Lü: Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22179030) and 21C Innovation Laboratory, Contemporary Amperex Technology Ltd (No. 21C-OP-202218).
